app.title: Arithmetic — From Adders to Execution
app.subtitle: Everything reduces to addition: how computers do math with just gates and wires
adders.intro: Addition is the foundation of ALL arithmetic in a CPU. Every subtraction, every multiplication, every comparison ultimately flows through an adder circuit. Here we build one from scratch: starting with a half adder (2 gates), scaling to a full adder (5 gates), and chaining them into a ripple-carry adder that adds multi-bit numbers.
adders.half.title: Half Adder — Adding Two Bits
adders.half.description: The simplest arithmetic circuit: adds two single bits. The sum is XOR(A, B) — it's 1 when inputs differ. The carry is AND(A, B) — it's 1 only when both inputs are 1. Called 'half' because it can't accept a carry-in from a previous column.
adders.half.ariaLabel: Half adder: XOR gate for sum, AND gate for carry
adders.full.title: Full Adder — Handling the Carry
adders.full.description: Extends the half adder by accepting a carry-in from the previous bit position. Built from two half adders and an OR gate: first half-add A and B, then half-add that result with carry-in. If either step produces a carry, the final carry-out is 1.
adders.full.ariaLabel: Full adder: two half adders plus OR gate for carry propagation
adders.ripple.title: Ripple-Carry Adder (4-bit)
adders.ripple.description: Chains 4 full adders together, with each carry-out wired to the next carry-in. Just like adding numbers on paper — column by column, right to left, carrying as you go. The carry 'ripples' from the least significant bit all the way to the most significant bit.
adders.ripple.ariaLabel: 4-bit ripple-carry adder: four chained full adders with carry propagation
adders.ripple.overflow: Overflow! Result doesn't fit in 4 bits.
truthTable.title: Truth Table
addition.intro: The adder circuit from Tab 1 is the only arithmetic hardware a CPU truly needs. Both subtraction and multiplication reduce to addition — no separate circuits required. This is one of the most elegant insights in computer architecture.
addition.sub.title: Subtraction = Addition with Two's Complement
addition.sub.description: To compute A − B, the CPU doesn't subtract at all. It negates B using two's complement (flip every bit, add 1), then adds: A + (−B). The same ripple-carry adder handles both addition and subtraction — just toggle a MUX and a carry-in wire.
addition.sub.ariaLabel: Subtraction as addition: two's complement transformation step by step
addition.sub.step3: Step 3 — Add A + (−B) using the SAME adder
addition.sub.insight: The beauty of two's complement: x + NOT(x) + 1 = 0 for any x. Therefore NOT(x) + 1 = −x. The ALU reuses the exact same adder for both ADD and SUB — it just needs NOT gates on the B input and a carry-in of 1. No separate subtraction hardware!
addition.mul.title: Multiplication = Shift-and-Add
addition.mul.description: Binary multiplication is like long multiplication on paper, but simpler: each digit is only 0 or 1, so each partial product is either 'add shifted value' or 'skip'. The multiplicand is shifted left and conditionally added to a running total — using the same adder circuit again.
addition.mul.ariaLabel: Multiplication as shift-and-add: long multiplication grid with step-by-step trace
addition.mul.insight: Each 'multiply by one bit' is just an AND gate (bit × multiplicand). Each 'add to running total' is a ripple-carry adder. Multiplication is nothing more than conditional additions of shifted values. A 4-bit multiplier needs at most 3 additions.
alu.intro: The ALU is where all operations come together. It takes two N-bit numbers and an operation code, routes them through the appropriate circuit (adder for arithmetic, gate arrays for logic), and produces a result plus four condition flags that the CPU uses for branching decisions.
alu.title: 8-bit ALU — One Circuit to Rule Them All
alu.unaryNote: NOT is unary — only uses operand A
cpu.intro: This is where everything comes together. A real Intel 4004 gate-level CPU executes instructions step by step. Every ADD goes through the adder from Tab 1, every SUB uses two's complement from Tab 2, and every operation flows through the ALU from Tab 3. Watch registers change, carries propagate, and programs run.
cpu.prog.count.name: Count to 6
cpu.prog.count.desc: Increments R0 from 0 to 6 using a loop with INC and conditional jump (JCN)
cpu.prog.add.name: Add Two Numbers
cpu.prog.add.desc: Loads 5 and 7 into registers, adds them (5 + 7 = 12), stores result in R2
cpu.prog.fib.name: Fibonacci Sequence
cpu.prog.fib.desc: Computes Fibonacci numbers using register-to-register addition: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13
cpu.prog.branch.name: Conditional Branch
cpu.prog.branch.desc: Compares 9 and 5 using subtraction, branches to store 1 (greater) or 0 (less) in R2
